# opt -passes=loop-interchange fails with "Instruction does not dominate all uses!"

**Author:** mikaelholmen
**URL:** https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/issues/160068
**Status:** Closed
**Labels:** loopoptim, crash, generated by fuzzer
**Closed Date:** 2025-10-20T09:23:37Z

## Body

llvm commit: cca769ac3f1
Reproduce with:
```opt -passes=loop-interchange bbi-110771.ll -o /dev/null```
Result:
```
Instruction does not dominate all uses!
  %cond329.us.us.lcssa = phi i16 [ %cond329.us.us, %for.cond.cleanup295.us ]
  %.us-phi.us = phi i16 [ %cond329.us.us.lcssa, %for.cond.cleanup295.split.us.us ]
LLVM ERROR: Broken module found, compilation aborted!
```

[bbi-110771.ll.gz](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/22464426/bbi-110771.ll.gz)

### Comment 2 - sjoerdmeijer

---

### Comment 3 - sjoerdmeijer

@mikaelholmen : I've just picked this up, and was wondering if you happen to have the input source code? It's a bit of a weird case. There is a phi node with no uses in a latch block, and I was wondering how we ended up with this. I.e., I am not sure if interchange should be resilient to this (probably), but I am also wondering if something else has gone wrong earlier. 

---

### Comment 4 - mikaelholmen

@sjoerdmeijer Yes, I have the original (generated) C program. However, it's in a C dialect only supported by our downstream compiler so it's not something you could use anyway.
I'll see if I can reduce the C input program though and share something more that makes sense.

Note that we've seen this with a non-standard opt pipeline ```-passes='function-simplification<O2>,loop(simple-loop-unswitch<nontrivial>,loop-interchange)' ```, so it's possible that it's only seen when some standard passes are not run.

---

### Comment 5 - sjoerdmeijer

Ok, great, thanks, also for the context. I think it now makes sense now what I am seeing. I will now start looking into interchange. 

Just dumping this here if it helps and maybe for completeness, here's the test case with reshuffled blocks and renamed variables and labels because I couldn't see what was going on with all the long block names:

```
define i16 @main() {
entry:
  br label %BB1

BB1:                ; preds = %BB5, %entry
  %i = phi i64 [ 1, %entry ], [ %i.next, %BB5 ]
  br label %BB2

BB2:                                ; preds = %BB3, %BB1
  %j = phi i16 [ 0, %BB1 ], [ %j.next, %BB3 ]
  %arrayidx.us.us = getelementptr i16, ptr null, i16 %j
  %0 = load i16, ptr %arrayidx.us.us, align 1
  %cond329.us.us = select i1 false, i16 0, i16 0
  br label %BB3

BB3:                                ; preds = %BB2
  %j.next = add i16 %j, 1
  br i1 true, label %BB4, label %BB2

BB4:                  ; preds = %BB3
  br label %BB5

BB5:                           ; preds = %BB4
  %UNUSED = phi i16 [ %cond329.us.us, %BB4 ]
  %i.next = add i64 %i, 1
  %cmp286.us = icmp ugt i64 %i, 0
  br i1 %cmp286.us, label %BB1, label %exit

exit:                     ; preds = %BB5
  ret i16 0

; uselistorder directives
  uselistorder i64 %i, { 1, 0 }
}
```

---

### Comment 6 - mikaelholmen

Ok, here is something. I don't know if this helps in any way but I'll attach some files.

Reduced C program containing two nested loops: r.c

Clang output (run for my target, then slightly cleaned): r.ll

Then I ran ```opt -passes="function-simplification<O2>" r.ll -S -o r2.ll``` for my target producing: r2.ll
(If I don't specify my target here something gets different in the output and it won't expose the crash in the next step.)

Now you can reproduce the crash with:
 ```opt -passes="loop(simple-loop-unswitch<nontrivial>,loop-interchange)" r2.ll -S -o r3.ll```

[r2.ll.gz](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/22489655/r2.ll.gz)
[r.ll.gz](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/22489653/r.ll.gz)
[r.c.gz](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/22489654/r.c.gz)
